From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British music magazine

*Uncut*

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Cover of the December 2024 issue | |
| Editor | Michael Bonner[[1]](#cite_note-1) |
| Categories | [Music magazine](/wiki/Music_magazine "Music magazine") |
| Frequency | Monthly |
| [Circulation](/wiki/List_of_magazines_by_circulation "List of magazines by circulation") | 47,890 (ABC Jul – Dec 2015)[[2]](#cite_note-Circ-2) Print and digital editions |
| First issue | May 1997 |
| Company | [Kelsey Media](/wiki/Kelsey_Media "Kelsey Media") |
| Country | United Kingdom |
| Based in | London |
| Language | English |
| Website | [uncut.co.uk](http://www.uncut.co.uk/) |
| [ISSN](/wiki/ISSN_(identifier) "ISSN (identifier)") | [1368-0722](https://www.worldcat.org/search?fq=x0:jrnl&q=n2:1368-0722) |

***Uncut*** is a monthly [magazine](/wiki/Magazine "Magazine") based in London. It is available across the English-speaking world, and focuses on music, but also includes film and books sections. A DVD magazine under the *Uncut* brand was published quarterly from 2005 to 2006. The magazine was acquired in 2019 by Singaporean music company [BandLab Technologies](/wiki/BandLab_Technologies "BandLab Technologies"), and was published by NME Networks[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) from December 2021[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) to August 2023, when the brand was sold to [Kelsey Media](/wiki/Kelsey_Media "Kelsey Media").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## *Uncut* (main magazine)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Uncut_(magazine)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Uncut (main magazine)")]

*Uncut* was launched in May 1997 by [IPC](/wiki/TI_Media "TI Media") as "a monthly magazine aimed at 25- to 45-year-old men that focuses on music and movies", edited by [Allan Jones](/wiki/Allan_Jones_(editor) "Allan Jones (editor)") (former editor of *[Melody Maker](/wiki/Melody_Maker "Melody Maker")*).[[8]](#cite_note-8) Jones has stated that "[t]he idea for Uncut came from my own disenchantment about what I was doing with *Melody Maker*. There was a publishing initiative to make the audience younger; I was getting older and they wanted to take the readers further away from me", specifically referring to the then dominant [Britpop](/wiki/Britpop "Britpop") genre.[[9]](#cite_note-PG-9)

According to IPC Media, 86% of the magazine's readers are male and their average age is 37 years.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

*Uncut'*s contents include lengthy features on old albums, interviews with film directors, music and film news, and reviews of all major new album, film and DVD releases. Its music features tend to focus on genres such as [Americana](/wiki/Americana_(music) "Americana (music)"),[[9]](#cite_note-PG-9) [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") and [alternative country](/wiki/Alternative_country "Alternative country"). Each month, the magazine includes a free [CD](/wiki/CD "CD"), which may include both new and older music. Special Issues have covered [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2"), [Radiohead](/wiki/Radiohead "Radiohead"), [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan"), [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen"), [the Byrds](/wiki/The_Byrds "The Byrds"), [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie"), [Demon Records](/wiki/Demon_Records "Demon Records"), [Eric Clapton](/wiki/Eric_Clapton "Eric Clapton"), [John Lennon](/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon"), [Pink Floyd](/wiki/Pink_Floyd "Pink Floyd"), [Queen](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)"), [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese"), [Motown Records](/wiki/Motown_Records "Motown Records"), [Morrissey](/wiki/Morrissey "Morrissey"), [George Harrison](/wiki/George_Harrison "George Harrison"), [Jimmy Page](/wiki/Jimmy_Page "Jimmy Page"), [Led Zeppelin](/wiki/Led_Zeppelin "Led Zeppelin"), [the Beach Boys](/wiki/The_Beach_Boys "The Beach Boys"), [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney"), [Neil Young](/wiki/Neil_Young "Neil Young"), [the Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"), [Elvis Costello](/wiki/Elvis_Costello "Elvis Costello"), [the Kinks](/wiki/The_Kinks "The Kinks"), [Fleetwood Mac](/wiki/Fleetwood_Mac "Fleetwood Mac") and more.

*Uncut* underwent a radical redesign in May 2006, as a result of which the magazine no longer catered for books and reduced its film content.

Jones writes a regular monthly column, recounting stories from his long career in music journalism.

*Uncut*'s monthly circulation dropped from over 90,000 in 2007[[9]](#cite_note-PG-9) to 47,890 in the second half of 2015.[[2]](#cite_note-Circ-2)

In 2019, TI Media, successor to IPC, sold *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")* and *Uncut* to Singaporean company BandLab Technologies.[[11]](#cite_note-11) BandLab Technologies became the Caldecott Music Group in 2021 and split itself into three sub-units called BandLab Technologies, Vista Musical Instruments and NME Networks, with the latter group continuing to publish *Uncut*. In May 2023,[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) NME Networks sold *Uncut's* print and digital assets to Kelsey Media, publisher of car magazines like *Car Mechanics*,[[14]](#cite_note-14) *Motorsport News*[[15]](#cite_note-15) and *Fast Car*, and farming titles like *Crop Production Magazine*, *Practical Pigs* and *The Smallholder*.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The first copy of *Uncut* published under Kelsey Media was the September 2023 issue (Take 317) in August, with the magazine still being sold in shops with its *Now Playing* compilation CD.

## *The Ultimate Music Guide*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Uncut_(magazine)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The Ultimate Music Guide")]

*Uncut* often produces themed spin-off titles celebrating the career of one artist. This series is now known as *The Ultimate Music Guide*, but was initially known as *Uncut Legends*. The series started in 2003 with an inaugural issue devoted to [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan"), edited by [Nigel Williamson](/wiki/Nigel_Williamson "Nigel Williamson") (though the majority of titles that followed were produced by magazine editor [Chris Hunt](/wiki/Chris_Hunt "Chris Hunt")), followed by magazines entirely devoted to Radiohead, [Kurt Cobain](/wiki/Kurt_Cobain "Kurt Cobain"), U2, Bruce Springsteen, [Tom Waits](/wiki/Tom_Waits "Tom Waits") and John Lennon (with the Lennon magazine being produced to commemorate the 25th anniversary of the death of the former member of the Beatles).[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Since the series was rebranded as *The Ultimate Music Guide*, artists such as [Kraftwerk](/wiki/Kraftwerk "Kraftwerk"), [Wilco](/wiki/Wilco "Wilco"), [Creedence Clearwater Revival](/wiki/Creedence_Clearwater_Revival "Creedence Clearwater Revival"), [the Fall](/wiki/The_Fall_(band) "The Fall (band)") and [Talking Heads](/wiki/Talking_Heads "Talking Heads")[[18]](#cite_note-18) have been featured, with updated and expanded versions of earlier guides also released as Deluxe Editions (for example *Lennon: 80th Birthday Edition*[[19]](#cite_note-19) or [PJ Harvey](/wiki/PJ_Harvey "PJ Harvey")'s guide which came out again as a '30th Anniversary Edition' in June 2021).[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In 2021, *Uncut* launched more spin-offs including *The Complete Bob Dylan*[[21]](#cite_note-21) and *The Beatles – Miscellany & Atlas*, the latter being a publication typeset in the style of [Schott's Miscellany](/wiki/Schott%27s_Almanac "Schott's Almanac").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### *The History of Rock*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Uncut_(magazine)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The History of Rock")]

In 2015, *[The History of Rock](/wiki/The_History_of_Rock_(magazine) "The History of Rock (magazine)")* (using a title previously used for an [Orbis Publishing](/wiki/Orbis_Publishing "Orbis Publishing") partwork) was launched as a spin-off from *Uncut*. The new magazine would cover the key events in rock history by year, starting in 1965 and continuing to the present day (though the series was never completed).[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-TimeIncUK-24) *The History of Rock* reproduced contemporary articles that had originally appeared in the music publications of IPC Magazines, with articles from *[Melody Maker](/wiki/Melody_Maker "Melody Maker")* and the *[NME](/wiki/NME "NME")* (used for the period 1965 to 1990) joined by articles from *[Vox](/wiki/Vox_(magazine) "Vox (magazine)")* and *Uncut* in later issues.[[24]](#cite_note-TimeIncUK-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### *Ultimate Genre Guide*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Uncut_(magazine)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ultimate Genre Guide")]

Another spin-off to Bandlab's *Ultimate Music Guide* is the *Ultimate Genre Guide*, which takes an in depth look at a particular music style. Magazines in this series include 2021's *Ultimate Genre Guide to Shoegaze*[[26]](#cite_note-26) which features articles about [The Jesus and Mary Chain](/wiki/The_Jesus_and_Mary_Chain "The Jesus and Mary Chain"), [Lush](/wiki/Lush_(band) "Lush (band)") and [My Bloody Valentine](/wiki/My_Bloody_Valentine_(band) "My Bloody Valentine (band)"), as well as issues devoted to Punk, Glam and Soul[[27]](#cite_note-27) In July 2023, the latest *Ultimate Genre Guide* was published, a title devoted to Goth which featured articles on the Cure, Nick Cave and cover star Siouxsie.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## *Uncut DVD*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Uncut_(magazine)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Uncut DVD")]

In late 2005, editor Allan Jones and publishing director [Andrew Sumner](/wiki/Andrew_Sumner "Andrew Sumner") launched a spin-off of the main movies and music magazine, that focused its attention on DVD releases of classic movies. Billed as "the only great movie magazine", *Uncut DVD* was designed to compete with such established titles as *Ultimate DVD*, *DVD Review* and *DVD Monthly*. Despite strong reviews in the UK trade press, *Uncut DVD* folded after three quarterly issues.

## Uncut Music Award

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Uncut_(magazine)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Uncut Music Award")]

In 2008, *Uncut* launched its inaugural Uncut Music Award; this is described as "a quest to find the most inspiring and rewarding musical experience of the past year". A list of 25 nominees is selected by a panel of 10 judges, who are all musicians or music industry professionals, and they come together to decide a winner.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Past winners have included [Fleet Foxes](/wiki/Fleet_Foxes "Fleet Foxes") (2008), [Tinariwen](/wiki/Tinariwen "Tinariwen") (2009), [Paul Weller](/wiki/Paul_Weller "Paul Weller") (2010) and [P.J. Harvey](/wiki/P.J._Harvey "P.J. Harvey") (2011).

## Notes
